questions.questionText: The most common type of renal stone is:
questions.optionB: Calcium oxalate stone (70-80% — radiopaque, most common overall)
questions.explanation: Renal stones by composition: Calcium oxalate: 70-80% (most common), radiopaque, envelope/dumbbell shaped crystal. Calcium phosphate: often mixed with oxalate. Struvite/MAP (magnesium ammonium phosphate): 10-15%, infection stones (Proteus — urease-producing), staghorn calculus, coffin-lid crystals. Uric acid: 5-10%, radiolucent (only stone invisible on XR), rhomboid crystals. Cystine: 1-2%, hexagonal crystals, AR disorder (cystinuria). Treatment: <5mm observe, 5-10mm medical expulsive therapy (tamsulosin), >10mm ESWL/URS/PCNL. Reference: Bailey & Love — Urolithiasis.

questions.questionText: Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) involves hypertrophy of:
questions.optionB: Periurethral/transitional zone (causes urinary obstruction — hesitancy, weak stream, frequency, nocturia)
questions.explanation: BPH: benign enlargement of periurethral/transitional zone of prostate. Symptoms (LUTS — lower urinary tract symptoms): obstructive (hesitancy, weak stream, incomplete emptying, straining, terminal dribbling) and irritative (frequency, urgency, nocturia). DRE: smooth, firm, enlarged prostate. PSA: mildly elevated (NOT specific for cancer). Treatment: mild → watchful waiting. Moderate → α-blockers (tamsulosin — relax smooth muscle), 5α-reductase inhibitors (finasteride — reduce prostate size). Severe → TURP (gold standard surgery). Prostate cancer: peripheral zone (>70%). Reference: Bailey & Love — BPH.

questions.questionText: Testicular torsion is a surgical emergency because:
questions.optionB: Twisting of spermatic cord cuts off blood supply → testicular ischemia/infarction if not detorsed within 6 hours (golden period)
questions.explanation: Testicular torsion: twisting of spermatic cord → venous outflow obstruction → arterial ischemia → testicular infarction. Peak age: neonatal and puberty (12-18 years). Presentation: sudden severe scrotal pain, nausea/vomiting, high-riding testis, absent cremasteric reflex, bell-clapper deformity (horizontal lie). Golden period: 6 hours (>90% salvage if detorsed within 6h, <10% after 24h). Negative Prehn's sign (elevation doesn't relieve pain — unlike epididymitis). Treatment: EMERGENCY surgical exploration + bilateral orchidopexy (fix both testes). Do NOT delay for imaging. Reference: Bailey & Love — Testicular Torsion.

questions.questionText: Wilms tumor (nephroblastoma) is the most common renal tumor in children and presents with:
questions.optionB: Asymptomatic abdominal mass (most common presentation — smooth, firm flank mass that does NOT cross the midline)
questions.explanation: Wilms tumor: most common renal malignancy in children (peak 3-4 years). Presentation: asymptomatic abdominal mass (usually discovered by parent during bathing). Does NOT cross midline (unlike neuroblastoma which can). Triad: abdominal mass + hypertension + hematuria. Associations: WAGR syndrome (WT1 gene — Wilms, Aniridia, GU anomalies, intellectual disability), Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome (WT2). Treatment: nephrectomy + chemotherapy (vincristine, actinomycin D, doxorubicin). Excellent prognosis (90% survival). Reference: Bailey & Love — Wilms Tumor.
